data.title: FirstCash Holdings: A Business Built To Last Through Good Times And Bad
data.description:
Introduction

With so much uncertainty in the US stock market as of late, I recently wanted to find some new potential holdings that wouldn't just survive in a volatile macro regime, but potentially thrive. That was what led me to FirstCash Holdings (FCFS), the largest operator of pawn stores in the United States.

While the idea of a pawn shop being a strong recession resistant idea is not earth-shattering, I was admittedly very impressed when I dived into their latest earnings report and historical performance. With the stock recently breaking out to new all-time highs while the S&P 500 still recovering from a steep April selloff, I believe the setup is still attractive and was enough for me to initiate a small starter position in the stock.

In this article, I want to explore my investment thesis and explain why I believe the company has a good chance to continue delivering strong results, despite an uncertain yet cautiously optimistic outlook for the market at large.

Company Overview

Headquartered in Fort Worth, TX, FCFS operates over 3,000 pawn locations in the U.S. and Latin America. The current state of the company was largely formed in 2016 through a merger between First Cash Financial Services and Cash America Inc., while a 2021 acquisition of American First Finance marked an entry by the company into the buy now, pay later space. In addition to stores across 29 US states, the company has stores in Mexico, Guatemala, Colombia, and El Salvador across dozens of different store brand concepts.

In a recessionary or stressed economic environment, pawn loans can behave like countercyclical income streams, and FirstCash has proven this out over multiple cycles. The current trade and currency backdrop only makes the story more compelling.

Investment Thesis: The Pawn Model is Lucrative and Countercyclical; the Stock Trades Defensively

The simplicity of the pawn business model is what makes it so powerful in volatile economic environments. A customer brings in a physical asset—jewelry, electronics, tools—and receives a short-term loan secured by that item. If the loan is repaid, the customer gets their item back. If not, the pawn store sells the item to recover the loan principal and interest. There’s no unsecured lending risk, no dependence on credit scores, and minimal exposure to default.

What makes this model inherently counter-cyclical is its alignment with economic stress. When traditional credit tightens or when household liquidity gets squeezed, more people turn to pawn shops. Demand for secured loans rises, and inventory naturally flows into the retail side of the business. The result is potentially higher loan volume, faster inventory turnover, and pricing power in both lending rates and resale margins. It’s a rare retail model that can grow both sides of its P&L when consumers are under pressure. That being said, it doesn't rely on a poor economy to produce strong financial results, as the strong growth of the company over the last 10 years has evidenced.

Better said, FCFS is a well managed business with strong unit economics that has compounded earnings in good times and bad, having recently posted exceptional results right at a time when consumers are becoming more pessimistic. With strong net margins, the pawn model proves to be much more lucrative than traditional big box retailers like Target (TGT), Walmart (WMT) or even superstar Costco (COST). This largely flies under the radar due to the company being classed as a Financial rather than a Retail company, although I believe it is appropriate to compare to both sectors when analyzing. It is this grey area that might explain why the company is not a household name along with its relative small size ($6B market cap).

Another interesting thing to note on the company is its strong track record of value accretive acquisitions, which has been done at both a small regular scale of acquiring single stores (one store in Las Vegas was purchased in Q1, for example) and large-scale acquisitions like the 2016 merger or the recently announced deal to buy H&T Group, the leading Pawn operator in the United Kingdom. When looking at the financials, it can be seen that the company uses cash on hand to make small acquisitions and open new stores, while they fund large acquisitions of entire corporate entities through a blend of equity and debt. This is why Gross Margins peaked in 2002 and Return on Equity has swung up and down wildly over the years. While the company has been quite aggressive in making acquisitions, the fact that ROE and Gross Margins have been ticking up since the 2016 merger indicates to me that the management team understands how to integrate new concepts in a way that ultimately creates shareholder value. The deal aggressive deal to acquire H&T is the latest piece of evidence that the FCFS growth story still has a long way to run, potentially.

Solid Financial Results and Track Record Not Fully Realized in the Valuation

The Q1 report was clean. EPS came in at $1.86, up 39% year-over-year. Revenue grew over 14%, with double-digit growth in both U.S. and Latin American segments. Same-store pawn loans increased 13% in the U.S. and 14% in Latin America in constant currency, and fee income growth was strong across geographies.

The margins remained intact. U.S. segment operating margins were 23.9%, and Latin America saw margin expansion to 26.7%. Retail sales margins also improved modestly, and the merchandise mix continues to tilt toward high-turnover electronics and gold.

When the most recent quarterly numbers are juxtaposed against the annual numbers over the last 10 years, it doesn't look as extraordinary, as the company has increased Gross Profits by more than 5 times since 2015, faster than the ~4.5x increase in revenue seen over the same period. Indeed, the gross profit expansion has not been linear when accounting for the large M&A deals the company has done over this time, but has edged up to new local highs in subsequent years each time, showing strong integration capabilities.

Despite very strong financial metrics and an all-time high stock price, the current valuation is still below 5 year averages, making it look highly attractive.

Currency and Tariff Setup: Peso Strength Is Already Showing Up

While not very important to our thesis in the immediate sense, FirstCash earns a large share of revenue and profits in Mexico, and guidance assumes currency neutrality and a prolonged period of further dollar strength. However, I actually believe that Mexico is a big beneficiary of trade tension with China and the Mexican Peso has appreciated meaningfully vs. the dollar both over the last 5 years and this QTD. If the peso continues to strengthen structurally over time, the company will benefit without having to raise expectations. While this isn't necessary to underpin a strong investment thesis, it is a nice bonus and a way for US-based investors to get exposure to Mexico and Latin America.

Secondly, FirstCash is not materially exposed to Chinese imports or tariff risk. While the tariff outlook is changing daily and the markets currently lit with enthusiasm of easing trade tensions, I am taking a more skeptical approach with my portfolio. The company's product flow is heavily skewed toward refurbished consumer electronics and secondhand gold jewelry, items that are largely sourced domestically or within the local region and sometimes at negative acquisition costs if acquired due to defaulted pawn loans. That makes FCFS one of the few retail-adjacent names with zero exposure to the tariff debate, at least in terms of first-order effects. If anything, if new merchandise recently imported gets marked up aggressively due to tariffs, FirstCash might actually benefit from the "trade-down" effect that commonly benefits Costco but in a differentiated manner.

In a macro environment where trade barriers are rising, the dollar is weakening, and consumer liquidity is tightening, FirstCash stands out as a structurally advantaged model with resilient features. Furthermore, with management continuously compounding cashflow into more locations and returning cash to shareholders, there is little reason to believe that the company doesn't continue to generate market-beating rates of return especially given its modest valuation multiple of less than 20 times, lower than the average S&P 500 company.

Risks

Investing in FCFS comes with several risks. One that sticks out is the acquisitive nature of the management team as discussed both historically and currently. While there is strong historical precedent for these acquisitions ultimately accreting shareholder value, integration risk is always a concern, particularly if the company relies heavily on issuing shares to help fund them. This risk is seen as a double-edged sword in my view as it can be the ultimate driver of shareholder value in the long term but cause financial swings in the interim.

Another risk that is pronounced through the operations in South and Central America and the planned acquisition of the UK business is currency risk. Earlier I mentioned that the near and intermediate term impact of this might be neutral or even positive given the current environment of potential pervasive dollar weakness, however it is not easy to predict currency moves. The company turning into more of a multinational will amplify the currency risk over time, but again this serves as a double-edged sword that can insulate and diversify US shareholders without having to directly hold foreign currency.

Lastly, another risk that was touched on earlier vaguely is that at least over the past year, the stock has traded with modest inverse correlation to the S&P 500 on a day-to-day basis. While the stock has still trended up over time, it is reasonable to expect that an investment in this stock will not merely perform as beta to the index but rather a defensive but compounding position. I believe that this risk is core to the investment thesis however and can drive material outperformance of the index, so I feel it as fairly benign or at least a risk I am willing and eager to take on.

All in all, the company presents risks that aren't too atypical from a small-cap growth stock. However, its "defensive compounder" properties do still make the risks associated with it a good fit in contrast with the rest of my portfolio.

Technical Analysis

The stock has recently broken out to new all-time highs, ultimately resulting in a very bullish looking chart. Perhaps more interestingly, the stock price has been an inverse mirror image of the S&P 500 over the last year, swing trading in the opposite direction for the majority of the time. The combination of these two anecdotal datapoints make the current chart look like a truly excellent setup to enter, despite the current price not screaming full-tilt value. While the stock has recently traded sluggishly with the broader market ripping on trade optimism, this behavior falls in line with the stock trading as a defensive safe haven. With such a strong track record for growth, it truly looks like a great technical entry point for this defensive compounder.

Why I Started a Position

This is one of the rare businesses where the operating model is directly aligned with the kind of macro stress I think we may be heading into. While a lot of the defensive positioning I have done over the last month has underperformed the market on trade optimism the last 2 weeks, I feel confident in doubling down on more uncertainty throughout the year here.

FirstCash is set up to escape the trade war noise and continue to attract investment dollars. Add in strong unit economics, real capital discipline, and a historically recession-resilient model, and I’m comfortable initiating a small position even after a strong YTD move.

I’ll consider sizing into it more aggressively if we get a pullback since this is one of my smallest positions currently, but I’m now watching this name much more closely.

Final Thoughts: Worth a Flier Position; Buy

FirstCash doesn’t get much attention, and that’s probably part of the appeal. It’s a simple business, but one that can thrive in complex environments. With earnings strength already showing up, macro tailwinds forming, and the stock still reasonably valued on forward earnings, I think the setup deserves a closer look.

data.title: Lundbeck Delivering On The Financials, But Needs Some Positive Clinical Drivers
data.description:
The last few months have not been the best for the shares of Danish drugmaker H. Lundbeck A/S (OTCPK:HLBBF) (OTCPK:HLUBF) (HLUNa.CO) (HLUNb.CO), as concerns about tariffs and challenges to prices in the U.S., coupled with a disappointing clinical update, have combined to drive a roughly 15% decline in the local share price since my last update, a little worse than the performance of the broader European pharmaceutical sector (as measured by the STOXX Europe Total Market Pharmaceuticals index).

Lundbeck is in a challenging place right now. While the underlying fundamental performance of the business is pretty good, there are legitimate concerns about what the U.S. administration’s new policies could mean for the company, and there won’t likely be any significant clinical read-outs until late in the year. The pipeline does support worthwhile upside from here, but this is a stock where investor worries about drivers outside of management’s control could outweigh positive financial results for a few more quarters.

Good Momentum In Q1

In terms of the here-and-now financial performance at Lundbeck, I can’t find many complaints. The company followed up better-than-expected Q4 ’24 results with Q1 '25 results that were well ahead of expectations and didn’t really have any concerning flaws. While the guidance boost may disappoint some (particularly given the magnitude of the beat), I think conservative guidance is prudent at this point.

Revenue rose 16% in local currencies, beating expectations by about 8%. Growth was driven by the company’s “Strategic Brands”, which grew 24% (9% better than expected), while the company’s legacy business saw 2% revenue erosion (still good for a 6% beat).

Vyepti, the company’s migraine drug, was the leader in terms of growth (up 62%, making up about 17% of total revenue) and performance relative to expectations (beating by 17%). Rexulti (up 28%, beating by 5%) remains the largest overall contributor to revenue (around 24%), while the Abilify franchise (up 16%, beating by 11% and making up 16% of revenue) and Trintellix (up 7%, beating by 6%, and making up 20% of revenue) did fine. Lexapro/Cipralex, long off patent in most major markets, saw 1% growth in the quarter and still contributes about 10% of total revenue.

Gross margin improved 170bp year over year to 82.6%, beating by 50bp, helped by scale benefits and lower amortization costs. Operating expenses rose 13% in local currencies, a little higher than expected, but the beat on gross margin nevertheless allowed for 33% growth in operating income (margin up three points to 27.2%) and 24% local currency growth in adjusted EBITDA (margin up 190bp to 34.9%), beating by 21%.

As I said, the revision to guidance was modest relative to the beat versus expectations this quarter. Management raised its revenue growth guide from 7%-10% to 8%-11%, while boosting the adjusted EBITDA growth guide from 5%-11% to 8%-14%. Variability in ordering patterns (stocking/de-stocking), as well as impending patent expirations for some products in some geographies seem to be the primary reasons for relatively restrained guidance at this point.

Rexulti Performing Well, But An Upcoming AdComm Remains An Important Catalyst

Just after the publication of my last article, Lundbeck announced that the FDA made a late decision to hold an advisory committee meeting to review the company’s application for FDA approval to add the post-traumatic stress disorder (or PTSD) indication to the Rexulti label (in combination with sertraline, an already-approved drug). With the Q1'25 release, we now know that the panel meeting will be held relatively soon - on July 18, 2025.

As has been the case for drug development for PTSD in general, Rexulti’s path so far has had its ups and downs, with the pivotal Phase III results seeing the fixed dose miss its primary endpoint, while the flexible dose met it.

While a well-designed study should help Lundbeck’s case, it’s tough to call this more than a 50–50 shot at this point. It’s true that PTSD is a badly underserved condition in the U.S. - there are around 13M people diagnosed with the condition and close to 2M being treated with pharmaceuticals for the condition - but it’s also true that the FDA has been unpredictable when it comes to drugs with mixed clinical data.

Assuming no major changes to Rexulti pricing, 5% share of the U.S. PTSD drug-treated patient market could be worth something on the order of $1.5B/year in revenue. Given that Rexulti has already grown to around 3% share of the U.S. Alzheimer’s-associated agitation market two years after approval (and that indication now makes up about 20% of Rexulti revenue), I do think there is solid potential for the PTSD indication to add $1B or more to Rexulti’s annual sales contributions in the coming years, provided of course that the FDA approves the application.

Clinical And Operational Unknowns Are Certainly Risks

Lundbeck’s pipeline isn’t in the best shape, with a sparse late-stage pipeline and elevated clinical risk, but the three drugs it does have in Phase II/III testing do all have meaningful financial potential for the company and shareholders.

Amlenetug started its first Phase III study (MASCOT) late in 2024 despite a mixed Phase II outcome (“trends” and “signals” of efficacy, but a failure on the primary endpoint), and the drug has received orphan drug designations in the U.S., EU, and Japan. Multiple system atrophy (or MSA), the lead indication for the drug today and the focus of the MASCOT study, is an awful disease with no treatments today and could become a multibillion-dollar drug near the end of the decade, but I’m always nervous when Phase II studies produce “yes, but…” results.

Lundbeck is also developing Lu AG09222 as a new treatment for migraine, and announced back in March that an interim analysis of its Phase II PROCEED study led the company to pursue new IV formulation dose-response information after finishing the subcutaneous portion of the study. It looks as though the Street has interpreted this to mean that the sub-q formulation hasn’t delivered the hoped-for results, and I believe this is part of the reason why the stock has been weak – while Vyepti has shown that there is a market for IV-administered treatments for migraine, the IV administration requirement definitely limits its market potential.

Last but not least is bexicaserin, which the company is developing for Developmental and Epileptic Encephalopathies (or DEEs), an umbrella term for a host of conditions that cause seizures. Early results from a small Phase Ib/IIa study have been encouraging, with greater than 50% seizure frequency reduction, and the market potential is meaningful ($1B-plus), but this is an area of drug development littered with late-stage failures, and it remains a high-risk program in my mind.

Beyond the clinical risk here, there are also potential operational risks. As I’ve said in the past, positive clinical outcomes may happen, but the expiration of patents on key drugs like Abilify Maintena, Trintellix, and Rexulti, will happen in the coming years, and management will have to navigate the impact those have on sales and margins, particularly as the company cannot cut back too far lest it risk endangering the market potential of its pipeline.

There’s also the more recent issue of tariffs and potential price controls in the United States. It remains to be seen what the administration can actually do with respect to drug prices without Congress’s involvement, but it is at the very least a threat given that the company generates about half of its revenue in the U.S.

The actual impact to Lundbeck is hard to gauge at this point, as the price discrepancies between the U.S. and Europe differ by drug (with a large difference for Rexulti, but less for drugs like Vyepti) as well as by market channel (the Alzheimer’s agitation indication for Rexulti, for instance, is more weighted to government-funded plans than the depression indication).

The Outlook

Not too much has changed in my model relative to my last update, as financial outperformance has been counterbalanced by what I expect will be increased operating spending in the coming years to support R&D and to maximize revenue (more spending on sales and marketing, in other words).

Summing together the next five years’ revenue, my estimates have changed less than 1%, and I’m looking for roughly flat revenue over the next five years as growth in franchises like Vyepti is offset by patent expirations. FDA approval for, and meaningful adoption of, Rexulti in PTSD would certainly offer some upside.

My long-term revenue growth estimate is likewise pretty flattish, largely because I use a risk discounting mechanism for pipeline candidates – in other words, if and when the clinical trials for drugs in the pipeline offer positive read-outs (increasing the likelihood of eventual approval/commercialization), the expected revenue contributions will rise. There are, of course, no guarantees here, and key pipeline failures would likely lead the company to pursue additional M&A/in-licensing to preserve revenue.

As far as margins go, I think management’s target of 30%-plus adjusted EBITDA margin over the next five years is attainable, and I’m expecting free cash flow margins in the high-teens, with upside into the low-20%’s over time. That should drive low single-digit FCF growth even on flattish revenues, with upside if and when the pipeline candidates prove out.

Between discounted free cash flow and a P/E approach driven off of what the market has historically paid for a given level of EPS growth, I believe Lundbeck shares are undervalued today, potentially by more than 35%. The major downside risks at this point would be clinical trial failures and the imposition of price controls in the U.S. that would meaningfully impact revenue.

The Bottom-Line

I do believe Lundbeck shares are once again too cheap, but I also believe that the shares need to see positive regulatory and clinical developments to get moving up again in a sustained way. A successful AdComm meeting for the PTSD indication for Rexulti would certainly help, but otherwise investors are going to have to wait a bit for thesis-altering clinical data. In the meantime, good financial performance will certainly help, but this is a name that’s going to require patience to work out.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: U.S. Government Bond Yields Extend Climb Amid Tariff Uncertainty
data.description:
The benchmark 10-year US Treasury yield climbed above 4.5% this week for the first time in nearly three months, as tariff threats, inflation fears and higher-for-longer interest rates triggered another sell-off in US government bonds.

The Treasury 10-year yield, which moves opposite prices, has risen about 50 basis points since President Donald Trump's April 2 announcement revealing steep tariffs on goods from nearly all the country's trading partners.

Government bond yields since early April have been on "the tariff roller coaster," said Padhraic Garvey, head of global rates and debt strategy at ING, as tariffs have been delayed, threats by Trump have been walked back and some details of preliminary trade talks have bolstered hopes of a trade policy reprieve.

But with the Federal Reserve unlikely to move on rates until the tariff impact starts showing up in government data, the bond market is largely anticipating the sell-off to continue amidst heightened fears of higher inflation and interest rates. The 10-year yield could soon near 5% for the first time since October 2023.

"I'd suggest there is a general tolerance this side of 5%," Garvey said in an interview. "We've been at 5% before in this cycle, so getting back there would not be a dramatic move. That said, shooting above 5% could indeed be problematic, and likely only tolerable if the economy is doing really well."

Much of the ongoing sell-off in government bonds has taken place in longer term yields. The 30-year yield, for example, fell by about 30 basis points from the start of Trump's second presidential term in January to his April 2 tariff announcement, but has climbed by about 40 bps since. The 20-year yield, which initially fell 33 bps, has climbed 42 bps since April 2.

Tariffs may not ultimately prove as inflationary as markets currently fear, according to John Luke Tyner, head of fixed income and portfolio manager at Aptus Capital Advisors. Yields will ultimately be driven by economic growth, with rate likely pushing higher if the US does not appear to be imminently entering a recession, and inflation expectations as the market adjusts for future inflation risks.

"I would think this generally should keep term premiums elevated," Tyner said in an interview.

Where the bond market is headed will hinge on how much the tariffs actually stick and for how long, as this will impact the future direction of inflation, the labor market and the Fed's decision on when to cut rates and by how much, said Patrick Leary, managing director with Loop Capital Markets.

"We will need to wait and see what develops, but in the meantime, fixed income investors are favoring the front end and belly of the yield curve and avoiding the long end," Leary said in an interview. "This is a sharp contrast to previous recession fear trades."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Blackstone Secured Lending: Near Double-Digit Yield Likely Secure, But Wait For Now
data.description:
Introduction

As a business development company ("BDC") enthusiast, I'm currently still holding my BDCs and will continue to do so, despite a potential recession in the coming months.

However, I do think some will continue covering their base dividends, but are likely to see downside as a result of expected lower interest rates.

Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (NYSE:BXSL), one of the top BDCs that could perform solidly in a potential recession, reported their latest earnings and their performance could be an indication of what could be coming.

In this article, I discuss Blackstone Secured Lending's latest earnings report, fundamentals, and why I'm re-iterating my hold rating as they could see further downside despite their strong fundamentals.

Previous Hold Rating

I last covered Blackstone Secured Lending this past March, downgrading the BDC from a buy to a hold due to heightened uncertainty. With recession fears picking up as a result of a potential trade war, I assumed BXSL's upside was limited.

Since then, their share price has held up relatively well, but is still down vs the overall market. BXSL is down close to 6% compared to the S&P (SP500), up 4.51% at the time of writing.

During Q4, the BDC reported a strong quarter in terms of investment commitments over $1 billion and fundings totaling $1.4 billion.

Net investment income missed analysts' estimates by $0.02 and dropped $0.07 from the previous quarter, amounting to $0.84.

Total investment income managed to grow a solid $10 million from the previous quarter and $49 million the previous year.

BXSL also had a solid balance sheet with leverage below the peer average and robust liquidity totaling $2.4 billion. They also received full investment-grade credit ratings from Fitch & Moody's last year as a result.

Lower Base Rates, An Obvious Headwind

As previously mentioned, I plan to continue to hold my current BDCs, but the macro environment is likely to drag their share prices lower.

So, for investors looking to buy since many have pulled back recently, you could potentially get a better entry point over the next 6 to 7 months.

Reason being the economy is seemingly starting to show cracks. Recently, GDP declined for the first time since 2022, an obvious sign that consumer spending is slowing.

Moreover, this would also likely lead to compressed spreads for BDCs and lower investment activity, especially if a recession comes to fruition like many are assuming.

The overall market reacted positively to news of a potential trade deal with the U.K. recently. Shortly after, the market jumped another 1,000 points due to a potential deal with China that saw both countries reduce tariffs for 90 days.

And while trade deals in the future could avoid us falling into a recession, negative growth in the upcoming quarter would mean a recession, by definition standards.

As a result, the FED would likely lower interest rates. Currently, the market is pricing in interest rates to be between 50 basis points lower by December of this year.

Moreover, with the recent report showing CPI cooling more than expected, this could be the green light the FED needs to lower base rates further than expected. Ultimately, meaning lower portfolio yields and interest income for BDCs.

And this would likely result in lower share prices as investors potentially flee as BDCs become less attractive.

On the flip side, future tariffs would mean potentially higher inflation, a positive for the sector. And this could be a reason the FED kept interest rates steady at their recent meeting, as they continue to monitor incoming data.

Tariff impacts could be short-lived, but slowing economic growth could force the FED's hand. The economy has continued to be somewhat resilient, but I think we see at least one cut this year.

However, this would still have a negative impact on BDCs due to their predominantly floating-rate portfolios. But during BXSL's recent Q1 earnings, the slowing economy and base rates 100 basis points lower are already having an impact.

Net investment income of $0.83 managed to beat estimates by a penny, while total investment income was in-line with the consensus at $358 million. This grew solidly from the previous quarter's $353 million and from $304 million year-over-year.

This was likely attributed to overall portfolio growth, which grew impressively from the previous year. BXSL's portfolio value stood at $12.8 billion, up from $10.4 billion in the previous year's quarter.

Borrowers grew from 210 companies to 284. During the quarter, they added 14 new borrowers while exiting six.

And while BXSL's portfolio growth is to be commended, especially considering the current macro environment, their overall yield and investments/fundings declined from the previous year and quarter, an obvious result of lower base rates and a slowing economy.

Their yield was 10.2%, down from 10.4% in Q4 and 11.8% in Q1'24. This is also likely the reason their bottom line declined from $0.84 in the prior quarter and $0.87 annually.

As a result, dividend coverage dropped from 113% to 108%, still solid in comparison to some of their larger peers. However, this is something investors should keep a close eye on. To be fair, of its peer group, BXSL had the highest coverage with the exception of Ares Capital's (ARCC) 112.5%.

Investments & fundings also dropped below $1 billion, breaking Blackstone Secured Lending's streak of 5 consecutive quarters seeing more than $1 billion. In Q1, commitments amounted to $756 million, down from $1.24 billion in Q4 and $1.17 billion year-over-year.

Fundings also retracted from $1.38 billion in the previous quarter to $689 million. But management stated they expect these to pick up in the back half of the year.

But if the economy continues to slow, which, I think, could be likely, these could continue to decline, impacting their financials and dividend coverage in the coming quarters.

However, with BXSL's investment-grade balance sheet, high first-lien exposure, robust liquidity, larger borrowers, and manageable non-accruals, I suspect they will maintain and continue paying their current dividend of $0.77.

Leverage crept during the quarter, in-line with the peer average of 1.19x. But this was still within management's targeted range of 1.0x - 1.25x.

Liquidity was plentiful, with $3.4 billion in cash & revolver capacity and $1.3 billion in total debt maturing in January & September of next year.

Non-accruals rose slightly year-over-year on a cost basis from 0.1% to 0.3% and were flat at 0.1% at fair value. But it was well below the peer average of 2.7% and 1.2% on a cost & fair value basis according to BXSL's management during the quarter.

The LTM weighted-average borrower EBITDA in the latest quarter stood at $210 million, up nearly 10% from the previous year's $193 million.

While larger borrowers don't necessarily equate to better performances in downturns, Blackstone Secured Lending Fund's steady non-accruals seem to be a testament to this and their overall credit quality.

Valuation

At the current price of $30.62 at the time of writing, Blackstone Secured Lending is much more attractive than they have been in the last few years with higher for longer interest rates. At the current price, this gives them a price-to-NAV ratio of 1.13x.

Here's how they compare to peers:

Ares Capital: 1.06x

Blue Owl Capital Corp (OBDC): 0.91x

FS KKR Capital Corp (FSK): 0.84x.

Currently, BXSL & ARCC trade at slight premiums to their NAVs while OBDC & FSK appear more attractive currently trading at discounts. The current 8.4% premium is slightly higher than their 3-year average of 5.03%.

And while I think BXSL's fundamentals command a premium, limited upside and headwinds from potential lower base rates makes them a hold as further downside is likely.

I expect BXSL to trade near the $30 range for the near-term, but they could see selling pressure in the latter half of the year, potentially creating a better entry point.

Risks & Bottom Line

As mentioned previously, lower yields from lower base rates will continue to put pressure on BXSL's dividend coverage. In turn, this will likely increase selling pressure as investors become worried about a potential dividend cut.

But due to their strong fundamentals and robust liquidity, I suspect Blackstone Secured Lending will maintain their current $0.77 dividend, at least for the near to medium-term.

Investors should continue to monitor non-accruals and net investment income in the coming quarters, as these could become impacted if the economy continues to weaken.

While a falling share price below NAV would likely prompt me to upgrade Blackstone Secured Lending Fund to buy, increased economic uncertainty and expected lower base rates keeps me at a hold for now.

data.title: Build-A-Bear: Growth Priced In, But Tariff Impacts Or Recession Risks Aren't - A HOLD For Me
data.description:
The past few years, especially since 2019, have been very transformative for the Build-A-Bear (BBW) business. The company has grown revenues from $339M in FY2019 to $496M in FY2024, increasing Operating Income from $1.5M in FY2019 to $66.3M in FY2024. This is the result of good execution of their well-designed multi-year strategy to (i) increase experience locations, (ii) transform the business digitally on both internal and customer-facing fronts, and (iii) incremental investments to support profitable growth, under which the company has shifted its focus on the very profitable Wholesale business model (more on this later). Although the company continues to stay on this path of execution, I believe the growth is priced in, while the potential impacts of tariffs and economic weakness are not. This leads me to recommend a HOLD on this stock.

Revenue Growth

With the FY2024 result release, management has guided to a growth of “mid-single” digits in FY2025 revenues (snapshot below). Part of the revenue increase is expected to come from an additional 50 locations at least during the year. A majority of these locations would be under the ‘partner-operated/wholesale’ model, driving the business’s Commercial revenues.

The Partner-operated or Wholesale model, falling under the Commercial segment, is the most profitable business model for the company in terms of margins and ROI. This model is capital-light, where the partner builds the location and owns it fully, while BBW sells the partner the inventory, supplies & fixtures, and IP license for third-party use. As per BBW's latest investor presentation, the segment reported the highest pretax margin (of 50.2%) in FY2024 and ROI exceeding 100%. I commend the management for their measured capital allocation and not chasing a “growth by any means” strategy – a value-destroying proposition.

Management also prefers opening these locations in hospitality and tourist places where BBW holds a higher leverage to increase prices. Given this, I expect BBW to open 36 partner-operated locations, 7 corporately managed stores (Retail) and 7 international franchises. With the growth in store count and my assumption of ~5.5% increase in Commercial revenues per location (since most of the third-party stores in FY2024 were opened in the second half of the year, providing some uplift in FY2025 revenues), I expect revenues to grow by ~4% to $516M in FY2025, in line with management’s guidance of mid-single digits growth in revenues.

The Tariff Impact

BBW's business has exposure to the U.S. tariffs by virtue of its sales to the U.S. To assess the impact of tariffs, I first estimate the amount of U.S. sales. For this, I split the amount of N.A. sales (86% of revenues in FY2024) by 90%-10% between the U.S. and Canada, respectively, based on the population split between the two countries. This equates to ~77% of the total revenue for the U.S.

Next, we look at the countries of production. In its Q4 2024 Conference Call, management noted that <50% of the inventory will be from China. We can assume the rest to be largely from Vietnam, since BBW sources its products primarily from these two countries, according to the FY2024 10-K. Given this, I assume a 30% tariff rate on goods from China and 10% on the rest.

Based on the above, I arrive at an after-tax Gross Profit impact of ~3.7% ($19M increase in COGS, after-tax) or $1.4 per share impact on the EPS. Calculations shown below:

Recession and Economic Slowdown Risk

See, the tariffs are already here. The tariffs imposed by the U.S. came into effect in early April at 10% for imports from most countries and 145% for imports from China. However, the 145% tariff on Chinese imports was recently reduced to 30%. The tariffs have already shocked supply chains, businesses, and the behaviour of both consumers and companies. So, we can expect some downside risks to the economy. Would there be any impact on the toy industry's sales in the case of an economic slowdown? The short answer is yes. Toys can experience lower sales in such times as these are 'discretionary purchases'. However, this category is more resilient than its discretionary peers. Some reasons for this are listed below:

  1. Parents may cut their needs first before cutting the child’s needs, and
  2. Toys (especially plush toys) can be good value for money, providing the child with fun in an affordable way (BBW’s Mini Beans is around a $10 price point).

The toy industry's resiliency, compared to other discretionary categories, is also reiterated by Hasbro’s CEO in its latest earnings conference call. In summary, BBW may experience weaker sales during tough economic times, albeit with less intensity, when compared to its discretionary counterparts.

Valuation

Let's look at valuation without the impact of any economic weakness or the impact of tariffs. As mentioned earlier, I estimate sales of $516M in FY2025 based on the net addition of 50 locations and a 5.5% increase in Commercial revenues per location. Next, I assume Adj. EBITDA of 16.7% in FY2025 compared to 16.3% in FY2024. From this, I arrive at a Fair value per share of $43.2 based on an EV/EBITDA multiple of 7.5x, slightly higher than its 3-year average of 6.3x. This leaves little upside from the current price of ~$42, without even including the potential impact of tariffs and an economic weakness. This suggests that the growth is already priced-in, and therefore I recommend a HOLD on this stock.

As mentioned before, I estimate an EPS impact of tariffs of ~$1.4 per share. Assuming that the tariffs impact COGS only, we can reduce $1.4 per share from the $43.2 per share fair value for every year the impact persists. Let's now assume that the impacts of tariffs would persist for only one year (tariffs discontinue by the year-end or prices are raised to counter the increase in COGS by year-end), then my fair value reduces to $41.8 per share. Thus, I reiterate a HOLD recommendation on this stock.

Risk to Thesis

A higher growth in net earnings driven by sustained operating margin increases, by price increases or cost reductions, or both, presents some risk to my thesis. However, margin increase by price hikes is unlikely since management seemed hesitant (but open) to do so when questioned on this during their last earnings conference call:

And like the last resort is really to continue to raise prices

Another risk is a potential increase in the stock’s multiple. The company's earnings power and cash flow profile have improved substantially over the past years, and one can make a strong case for the increase in multiples. Rising Wholesale revenues over time allow for higher profitability and cash flows, and management's ability to increase both buybacks (shares reduced by 5% annually over the past three years) and dividends. Every 0.5x increment in EV/EBITDA raises Fair Value by $3.2 per share.

Conclusion

BBW has indeed transformed its business over the past few years, a notable achievement by the current management to execute a well-designed and effective strategy. The management has exhibited capital discipline by focusing its investments in higher ROI businesses and returning surplus capital to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks. However, I am recommending a HOLD on the stock, and the current stock level leaves no upside to my fair value estimate of $43.2 per share. This estimate is quite optimistic as it incorporates revenue growth of mid-single digits (in line with guidance) and does not account for tariff impact (cost hikes, supply chain disruptions and potential economic weakness).

data.title: JAAA: Underperformance Corrected, Warning Stays
data.description:
Introduction

It's been an unusual year for the Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (NYSEARCA:JAAA), as noted in my last update on it in March. Essentially, the fund had a rough patch where it was volatile, causing it to underperform its peers and the benchmark index it tracks.

This primary had to do with a discount forming, which I described as "persistent and deepening." It lasted for a lot longer than many investors were comfortable, which can be seen in the comments section of that last article.

To the ire of many investors, the fund was discounted to its NAV by over 1%, seemingly inconsistent with the claim of many that JAAA is suitable for cash.

I am among that group that has called JAAA as good as cash in the past, and for the most part, it is. This is because of its very high credit rating assigned to its holdings, which have a long history of resilience in past resilience to adverse credit events like the Great Financial Crisis ("GFC").

While JAAA wasn't around in the GFC, we can track it back to late 2020, and it's returned nearly-22% to shareholders since then. It is evident in the chart where the discount and adverse market conditions affected the fund in this chart, which goes to show how rare of an event it was, and also how unexpected by investors it was when it was unfolding.

Before we get into why I still think this is true (with some caveats), let's catch the new folks up on JAAA.

What's an AAA CLO?

If you're not new to this fund, you can skip down to the next section. For those not in the know, here is the description I gave in my last article:

CLOs are [investment] vehicles that bundle loans together to create diversified baskets of risky assets, thus reducing the risk. These risky loans are typically senior secured debt, meaning they are backed by assets of some kind, by companies that [typically use them for leveraged buyouts]. It's not that the CLOs are risky within the structure; they get paid out first, especially AAA investors. It's that the companies that issue the loans CLOs buy up are inherently risky businesses by the virtue of them needing to issue floating rate debt. It is typically less preferable than fixed rate debt, at least in our past decade of low rates...

A big positive for CLO investors is that they are paid out first in any event of default, since the loans they pull from are at the top of the capital structure.

Here are the tranches of the CLO market and their 1993-2023 default rates. Note that there has never been a default of a CLO in the AAA or AA tranches since the inception of CLOs 30 years ago.

JAAA invests in the safest CLOs, with 99% of their holdings in the AAA tranche, making it a great vehicle for cash. It typically experiences low volatility, and little to no capital losses. While it is more aggressive than money markets and CDs, JAAA represents a great place to store "dry powder," or cash set aside for future investment opportunities.

Compare CLOs 12-month drawdowns from 2012-2024, and we see a remarkably lower volatility asset than most debt instruments.

JAAA Holdings

One thing to note is that the holdings shown to investors in JAAA do not reflect what is actually in the underlying CLOs, as all investors can see from the details are the managers who have packaged and sold these loans. This is part of the "black box" component of CLOs, where it's difficult from an outsider's view to parse them.

Because JAAA's managers regularly participate in the "primary market," meaning that they are privy to the deal making process of the CLOs they invest in, one just has to trust management to deliver on this end.

Here is what the top ten holdings look like:

The fund holds 384 positions, with a 1-year trailing turnover rate of 58%. It does carry some duration, at 0.21yr, and its weighted average maturity is 4.66yr.

These CLOs all pay above-market rates, and the AAA tranche is paid out first, which has allowed JAAA to keep its yield and dividends high. Here is JAAA compared to a similarly priced (also $50/share) ETF that holds a similar duration (zero), but doesn't carry the excess risk of CLOs bringing up the yield.

The Discount is Gone, Back to Business

Since I published that previous article, the discount has evaporated, leaving us back at the normal 0% discount to NAV.

Note that ETFs are unlike CEFs in that they have a mechanism to level out discounts and premiums, making them less likely to develop deviations from NAV generally, and far less likely for these deviations to persist for long periods of time.

This was important for JAAA, as it rebounded quickly from its discount. While it did impact price, it's important to note that the fall sustained by JAAA through March and April was primarily caused by this discount forming.

The underlying loans did lose some value too, as economic uncertainty rising following the 'Liberation Day' announcement that rocked stocks around the same time caused spreads to widen.

Janus Henderson noted this in their Q1 update video as the primary cause, and explained what they are doing to address this:

We've long favored adding issuance from the primary market to add more carry and call protection to the portfolio, and to gain the ability to influence deal doumentation...We came into the year overweight spread duration as a result, which modestly detracted on a realtive basis as spreads widened.

While we remain overweight spread duration, and continue to add issuance from the primary market, we also added some shorter profiles over the quarter...

CLO spreads have widened about 20bp during the quarter, and continued to widen modestly into April as CLOs and all spread products have priced in higher economic uncertainty.

We've seen the CLO market proving resilient and behaving as expected during this year's market volatility.

While the price is still down compared to its former peak, the fund is back above the $50.50 mark, signalling that it is back to some level of normalcy, despite these spreads widening, which has caused the CLOs inside JAAA to lose some value, and thus JAAA's NAV erodes slightly.

Rates Will Remain Elevated

One of the core reasons to be bullish on AAA CLOs is because they derive their rates from a formula that is usually "SOFR + 200-900bp," and SOFR is elevated compared to recent history. The rising rates of 2022-2023 and still largely in effect, with central bank rates having only come down 100bp since its peak in 2024.

We can see this when we chart JAAA's TTM yield and SOFR. We can see a bit of lag, which is to be expected.

For those not in the know, "SOFR" is the Secured Overnight Financing Rate, essentially the rate that one can borrow cash at overnight, collateralized by US Treasuries. This rate is the market-going rate, but is very closely tied to the Federal Funds Rate, which is set by the Federal Reserve.

This means, if we can see where the Fed is going to move rates, then we know where SOFR will go, which subsequently tells us what JAAA will yield.

Recently, I covered the Federal Reserve FOMC meeting, where the Fed announced their decision to hold rates steady on May 8th. In this meeting, the Fed gave several reasons that they are holding rates steady, and likely will be moving forward. I recommend checking out that article if you're interested in the full details, but here are is the article's conclusion on the Fed's potential for rate cuts.

Ultimately, the Fed gave investors what they were expecting: rates are being held, and the current rate is considered as neutral as the Fed is willing to go currently. This is given that inflation is still above its 2% target, and the unemployment rate is steady at "natural" levels (~4%).

What surprised me was how much the Fed has backed off the language of the neutral rate being far lower than our current rate. While this is still true, discussing it less and less works toward tamping down investor expectations of easy-money policy being a default for the Fed.

Right now, the Fed is continuing to be in "wait and see" mode, not budging from their current rate. This may persist for some time and will likely only change once there are material changes in inflation below the 2% target or a largely negative shift in unemployment.

The major upside? Cash is going to keep paying well for a while, as its yield is largely dependent on the Fed Funds rate.

In that last line, I address directly what to do about this issue with the Fed, load up on high-paying cash rates, like AAA CLOs. Because the Fed has given clear guidance that they do not intend to rapidly ratchet down rates, and actually prefers to keep rates where they are for now, that means we should be able to enjoy the yields on floating rate instruments for some time as well, likely another full quarter of rates being held.

The first cut the futures market is expecting currently is in September, and they are pricing in a 25bp cut to the rate. Note that the probability has been going higher as time goes on. A month ago, a 25bp cut by September has a 13.3% change. Now, post the May 8th meeting, it's at almost 50%.

There is still a 30% chance that the Fed doesn't budge by then, and that has been growing rapidly, up from 1.6% chance a month ago.

The last time I covered JAAA, the odds were skewed much farther toward sooner rate cuts, making the fund look less attractive as expected dividends would decline if rates fall. If we go back to the ZIRP ("zero interest rate policy") era, JAAA will not be nearly as attractive, as its yield would likely fall below what one could get on similarly rated fixed-rate bonds.

Risks Remain for Timing-Based Investors

JAAA remains as a piece of my cash allocation, and a large part at that, despite the mishap with the discount and detriments to the fund brought about by widening credit spreads. I still recommend it as a solid place to park cash long term, as these kinds of issues are likely to remain temporary, even if they come up again.

However, as noted by the comments section in my last article (and likely this one too, depending on when you are reading this), the kinds of drawdowns that ETFs are capable of (discount related) that doesn't happen with money market funds, CDs, or outright holding USTs, may be too much for some folks' risk tolerance. This is also true for the fact that NAV can decline, although large losses have never been sustained in JAAA's history, they are possible and more probable than the collapse of the Treasury market.

We saw this back in 2022-2023, when rates were rapidly pulled up, as JAAA's NAV fell over 3% from its high.

It is for this reason that investors should consider JAAA an allocation to cash instead of "dry powder" ready to be deployed at a moment's notice. In adverse market conditions, we can see that spread products like CLOs, tend to underperform risk-off assets like T-Bills, largely because adverse market conditions cause spreads to widen.

If you are hoping to sell JAAA at the bottom of the market crash to shore up funds for "buying the dip," you're likely better off in a T-Bill fund like SGOV. This is true for basically all AAA CLO funds, not just JAAA. Looking at some of its competitors, like PGIM's PAAA and iShares' CLOA, show us that these funds were also adversely affected YTD by similar factors (aside from the discount issues JAAA faced).

Suitability

JAAA and other AAA CLOs still seem suitable as standalone allocations to the asset class, and can still be compared to cash, given the level of risk to default and repayment. In fact, AAA CLOs carry a higher credit rating than the US Government. That being said, they are subject to more adverse market conditions than US Treasuries, especially those on the shortest end like T-Bills, which are often also compared to cash. There is not much more risk on the table compared to T-Bills with AAA CLOs, but it's more reflected in the prices of the CLO ETFs than it is in the T-Bills ETFs.

This may scare off some investors, whose risk tolerance is far more in line with money market conditions than AAA CLOs. If a 1-3% decline in your cash position, even if explainable and temporary (as we've seen in the past with JAAA), causes you significant worry, this fund (and subsequently all CLO funds) are not for you.

However, there are investors who want to add low duration, floating rates with low risk to their income portfolios, and are willing to accept those kinds of discounts and NAV declines in order to benefit from the significantly higher dividend rate. JAAA is the flagship AAA CLO ETF on the market for a reason, and that is because it has one of the longest track records and AUM.

The past discount issue should give investors some pause, and is a great case for incorporating a diversity of CLO ETFs, which may invest in different AAA tranches, like the aforementioned PAAA and CLOA. There is a great case to be made here about not keeping all of your CLO eggs in one basket, even if it itself is diversified, because of the ETF-wide mechanics that can cause their own issues.

There is effectively no cap on how much JAAA one could hold, as it is very similar to a cash position (with the caveats of the discount, NAV, etc.,) and so is suitable for all investors.

That being said, note that it is not suitable for all conservative investors, especially those who are sensitive to changes in the price of the fund during adverse market conditions like crashes, where the fund has historically taken detours from its normal grind upwards.

Conclusion

Ultimately, the Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF has recovered from its discount, which was deep and persistent for a time, especially during the recent market turmoil in April. Now that it has recovered, we can see that there was also pain in the NAV itself, as markets are pricing in more economic uncertainty and the potential for the leveraged buyouts that fill most of the CLOs in JAAA's holdings to go kaput. While those market conditions in the past, such as the Great Financial Crisis, hadn't shaken out the AAA tranches, that risk always remains.

What this last episode showed us more than anything is that there is a cohort of very conservative investors who fret over the small changes in price during these times, and those investors are far better served by allocating to T-Bills and accepting the lower yield.

For me, I do not park my "dry powder" in JAAA, and instead I am willing to hold it through those adverse periods while it recovers. I advise others to consider keeping "dry powder," money ready to deploy into new opportunities, in safer funds like T-Bill ETFs.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: TPG's $2.2 Billion Bid For AvidXchange
data.description:
The future of AP Automation

The world of fintech can be boring but profitable when the markets pick up. In the past few months, I wrote about the Enfusion buyout and MoneyLion M&A deals, which have held steady. Today I will cover AvidXchange (AVDX), which is being acquired for $2.2 billion in cash. The buyers are TPG (TPG) and Corpay (CPAY), both credible, larger cap public companies, which increases the certainty of this deal closing.

Before diving into the world of merger arbitrage, it’s important to understand the fundamentals of the buyers and sellers. Otherwise, you can easily fall into the trap of picking up pennies in front of the steamroller. That’s why when deals get announced and the spreads tighten, you need to invest in ideas with a high probability of closing. Which is why I use websites like Inside Arbitrage by Asif Suria to follow for deal updates.

So what does AvidXchange do?

AvidXchange makes money from accounts payable automation. This is a very boring business, but I have written about some adjacent businesses in the past. I recommend checking out my past articles on BlackLine, OneStream and Intuit. The common theme of all these companies is that they provide finance & accounting software solutions.

AvidXchange’s business model differs in one way from the names mentioned above because they focus more on payment solutions. Payments is a mission-critical segment for every business, which makes the fundamental value of AvidXchange very strong. AvidXchange’s flywheel below will give you a good snapshot of their business.

AvidXchange digitizes and automates the accounts payable workflow, which includes invoice management and bill payments. As you can see in the flywheel above, they facilitated payments to over 1.35 million suppliers.

AvidXchange’s first quarter results were released last week, and the company is delivering low-single digit growth in revenue and total transactions processed. Low growth combined with a cash-rich balance sheet and depressed stock price is the perfect target for any private equity buyer.

The Buyers and Deal Details

On May 6, 2025, TPG and Corpay announced they will acquire AvidXchange for $10.00 per share in cash, valuing the company at $2.2 billion. TPG will hold a majority interest through its TPG Capital platform, while Corpay will acquire a minority stake with an option to buy the remainder in 2028.

For context, Corpay is a corporate payments company that provides a range of payment and expense management solutions for businesses and consumers. You might know them as FleetCor Technologies, before they rebranded in March 2025. Corpay also focuses on accounts payable automation and streamlining payment solutions for customers. Today they operate in 100 countries to serve 800,000 clients worldwide. Acquiring a minority stake in AvidXchange makes sense given how adjacent their businesses are.

As for TPG, they are a well-known private equity firm with experience growing technology companies. Similar to any other PE shop, TPG is focused on top-line growth, paying down debt and extracting profits. Working with a strategic buyer like Corpay is not usual, so TPG is pricing in synergies since the payments businesses are complementary. If you want to learn more about cross-sales payment synergies, I recommend reading my recent article on Shift4 and their acquisition of Global Blue.

Also, certain members of the AvidXchange senior management team have agreed to roll over a significant portion of their equity in support of the transaction. The transaction was unanimously approved by the independent members of the Board of Directors of AvidXchange. The deal is expected to close in the fourth quarter of 2025.

The Risk and Reward of M&A

Right now, AvidXchange trades at $9.75 per share. TPG and Corpay will acquire the shares for $10.00 in cash, so you stand to make $0.25 or 2.56% on this deal. Unless you are a risk arbitrage shop, you probably don’t want to risk capital to buy these shares now. Even if you own the stock today, it might make sense to sell the stock before the deal closes in Q4 2025.

Locking up your capital for the next 6–9 months won't make sense for such a tiny spread. It’s also unlikely that another bidder will come along. TPG is a credible investment shop and Corpay has more than enough cash to close their minority stake.

Instead, it’s better if you sell out at $9.75 and reinvest the proceeds elsewhere. Without levering up, you can buy treasuries or even the index to generate a 3-4% return over the next 6–9 months.

data.title: Antero Resources Now Decisively, Financially In The Black (Upgrade)
data.description:
Antero Resources (NYSE:AR) reported stronger quarterly cash flows than during the past few years. The company repurchased stock and paid down a significant portion of its debt. This reflects a very positive outlook for its business going forward. When considering comments in the previous call, management stated its priorities for cash flows placed debt repayment ahead of every other opportunity. Yet, they did both, repurchased stock and retired debt. This shows their strong positive posture, with still $300 million left in debt to extinguish. The income sheet turned black. This continues our coverage of Resources. Our last writing, discussed trading with natural gas demand cycles. Now, well, yes, it is hard to see things in the black, but would you humor us and come look. Maybe, just maybe, we can squint really hard and see something.

Quarter Results

From the 1st quarter presentation, we include several slides. The 1st shows the cash flow.

The second and third show debt.

Although there is no short-term debt payments required, the next slide shows the amount left on the credit facility now at $300 million, approximately one quarter in cash flow.

Other important results included:

  • High premium vs. Mont Belvieu year over year with C3+ NGL prices pegged at up to $2.50.
    • To the extent that the liquids portion pricing trends with crude oil, and in the past, it has, weaker crude prices will be a headwind.
  • Production of natural gas remained constant even with lower capital costs.

The company is now in very good financial shape. In a few more quarters, the last portion of debt, the $300 million plus a lot more shares of stock, will likely be gone.

Collar Hedge

The next announcement from management was an interesting approach for this group. They announced a collar for hedging future natural gas production beginning in 2026. A slide included next summarizes the agreement.

This collar approach helps take advantage of what is left of the untapped wells, especially in the Haynesville region.

Also, we wrote in Evaluating Predicted Stair Stepped Natural Gas Spot Prices And More about the natural progression of natural gas pricing. We wrote:

We continue writing on NG with our last writing, Natural Gas' Hidden Future Of Muted Rising Prices, highlighting the existence of spare capacity, wells drilled, once in use, now idled during the recent production glut. We predicted prices would follow a stair step approach in their return to higher values for the next few years. Guessing the actual height of each stair is and was a dubious task with many unknowns."

Next, a slide from Antero Resources helps understand the range.

For Haynesville to really kick in, the traditional swing region, the price must be above $3.5.

The financial effect guarantees at least $100 million cash at the low end and as much as $400 million max. The collar will likely pay for approximately half of next year's capital spending. The spending for 2025 is guided at approximately $600 million.

Marketplace

Continuing with the marketplace discussion, a very good back and forth between Neil Mehta of Goldman Sachs, and Michael Kennedy, company CFO, discussed Resources view:

[W]e think Haynesville is probably $4 plus, and that's probably the inventory right now, and that runs out pretty quick. So I mean, no one really talks about inventory fatigue, but you'll definitely see it across the basin. So all this gas supply is going to have to come from not your Appalachian low-cost producers. It's going to come from ever-increasing second-tier acreage and second-tier basin. So that price will continue to go up.

Management, also, included a slide illustrating the value of being in the very prime of prime locations. A mother-lode worth of new demand is locating right smack dap in the middle of or adjacent to Resources' properties.

With the data center build out, the close proximity for relatively inexpensive energy bodes extremely positive for future markets.

Regarding Resources bullish stance on the Marcellus and other northern regions, MPLX (MPLX) management stated:

However, we continue to see robust production across our Marcellus, Utica and Permian operating regions. These basins have some of the lowest breakeven prices in the U.S., offering economically advantaged and development opportunities. Based on feedback from our producer customers, we continue to expect year-over-year volume growth."

Antero management added their opinion:

The extensive resource base of the Marcellus and Utica Shales provides certainty of long-term natural gas supply while supportive state regulations are leading to fast approvals and attractive incentives to build in the region."

With premier resources, the company sits in the investor's catbird's seat.

Risk & Reward

Antero Resources is an extremely well-run company positioned in the best of the best natural gas resources inside the U.S. It processes little debt with a leverage of 1.1 and heading lower. The free cash flow for 2025 was estimated at approximately $1 billion at the end of 2024 report. Into the future, growth in market size looms positive. When asked about stock repurchases verses debt repayment, Kennedy offered this:

Yes, John, yes, we're opportunistic and we're trying to be countercyclical. We didn't think we'd get this opportunity to buy shares at these prices. I mean, when you look at our fundamental outlook, it's as strong as ever. And to have the shares valued at where it is today is an opportunity for us. So that's why we pivoted towards share buybacks. We have a lot of visibility into our business and our cash flow generation and we don't really have any debt, so very little debt."

Management believes that the stock is dirt cheap.

What seems most interesting for us and maybe for other long-term investors is what happens when the company exhausts all of the debt and most of the stock? Do they buy something with the massive cash flows? Do they pay huge dividends once again? Or will they balance the two? The business will generate enough cash to buy almost all the debt this year, that is if they choose. Over the next some years, at $40 per share and $2 billion a year in cash flows, they could purchase all of the stock. When is enough, enough? Do they purchase half or two-thirds? Or?

The important driving force for investing seems related mostly to price and seasonal swings. We have traded the company twice in the past few months resulting in nice profits but are sitting on the sidelines for now. If the price drops lower and offers a technical entrance point, we plan to rebuy. Our gut feeling suggests that the summer season is close enough for a long cycle entrance. For those with long-term investing intentions, the recent price in the high 30s probability represents a good entrance. We rate this a buy with some level of caution that lower prices might appear.

The major risk, in our view, is once again too much production. Recent EIA storage and production reports show that storage is at or slightly below the 5-year average, and that production is slowly slowing down. These two parameters are by far the most important to watch. Management expects Haynesville production to continue acting as the swing region, with at least $4 spot price to maintain that function. This dampens risk.

Now what did you see in the black? Actually, we saw a lot, a lot of very positive indicators. We certainly hope you did.

data.title: eGain Q3: Slower AI Knowledge Management Growth, But Outlook Points To Strong Rebound
data.description:
Investment thesis

eGain's (EGAN) Q3 results showed the company falling short of revenue guidance, yet beating on earnings expectations. When it comes to my bullish thesis for the stock, in my previous write-up I had stated:

If there are signs that AI knowledge management is not gaining traction in what is a highly competitive market, that would severely dent my bullish thesis.

When I look at how the AI knowledge management segment performed during this quarter, it is clear that year over year ARR growth of 11% was much weaker than expected. Despite finding this concerning, I do, however, see other positives, including management's expectation for growth to return to the high teens for the next quarter. With the company continuing to maintain solid profitability and shares trading at just 2.5 times AI knowledge management revenue, I still find the current valuation compelling enough to maintain my Buy rating.

Soft quarter, but the outlook remains promising

Despite outperforming with respect to earnings, management lowered its outlook for full-year revenue. While much of this was attributed to lower margin professional services revenue, there was also an element of weakness associated with AI knowledge management SaaS ARR which saw its year-over-year growth rate decelerate to 11%, compared to 17% in the prior quarter. Given that AI knowledge management revenue, which represents around half of eGain's total revenue, forms the crux of my investment thesis, I need to dig into the reason for this weakness and how it may play out going forward.

Though management did not clearly state the reason for this sharp deceleration, a closer look at the net retention rates show that there was either higher churn or reduced usage by existing customers, as this metric dropped from 99% in Q2 to 97% in Q3. While I acknowledge that this is indeed concerning, what outweighs it is the fact that management is guiding for growth to return to the high teens next quarter. A crucial factor underpinning this is the expansion with a top four US bank, likely JPMorgan Chase (JPM), a case study which is described in detail on eGain's website. Expanding with such as significant customer not only helps due to the higher revenue contribution, but also proves that the company's solution is finding good traction in a competitive market.

On the whole, while the sharp deceleration in growth this quarter dented my bull case, I believe that the improved outlook and the signing of a major client of this magnitude, which follows the signing of one of the largest global airlines in Q2, keeps the bull case very much intact. The company is set to benefit from strong industry tailwinds with forecasts calling for a 43.7% CAGR for AI knowledge management systems until 2034. Additionally, headwinds from the loss of two large legacy business customers will diminish going forward as they have now been lapped, which should support a return to topline growth for total revenue.

Balancing valuation with the risks I see

Turning to valuation, I continue to assign no value to eGain's stagnant conversation and analytics segments, and instead solely focus on its AI knowledge management business. Based on AI knowledge management revenue for FY25 of approximately $43 million, shares are currently valued at a revenue multiple of 3.4. The company has a net cash position after deducting its current liabilities of close to $40 million, which when factored into its valuation, brings the revenue multiple down to just 2.5.

I find this valuation to be highly attractive given that growth rates are expected to accelerate to the high teens, while the company continues to remain solidly profitable despite its ongoing growth investments. In comparison, a peer like NICE (NICE) is valued at nearly 3.5 times revenue, while posting mid-single digit growth rates.

Despite the accelerating growth and relatively cheap valuation of EGAN stock, investors should closely track its retention levels in the coming quarters, to ensure that churn is not increasing. Moreover, the AI knowledge management space remains highly competitive, especially with some players like Verint Systems (VRNT) integrating their solutions together with their contact center offerings.

While I chose not to delve deeper into the conversation and analytics segments, their revenue and earnings have remained stable, and investors need this stable performance to continue in order for management to fund the growth initiatives related to AI knowledge management.

Conclusion

eGain's Q3 earnings brought about a mixed picture where AI knowledge management ARR decelerated sharply, yet management expects a sharp rebound next quarter, helped by a significant customer win. The current valuation remains appealing given the promising growth outlook, which makes me stick with my Buy rating for the stock.

data.title: EM Bond Performance Remains Solid In 2025
data.description:
The VanEck Emerging Markets Bond Fund was up 1.39% in April, compared to up 1.51% for its benchmark. Year-to-date, the fund is up 5.94%, compared to 4.84% for its benchmark. During April, China led outperformers; the team continues to see the worst of the Chinese property crisis as behind us. Underweights in Czechia and Malaysia led underperformers. We remain cautious on US dollar (USD) duration, USD spread duration, but open on local-currency duration. The fund has around 60% in curated local currency, 40% in USD bonds, with a noteworthy underweight to Brazil and South Africa in local currency. Carry is 8.2%, YTW is 9.9%, and duration is 5.3.

Last month we attended the IMF Spring Meetings. Read our key takeaways here.

Average Annual Total Returns* (%) (In USD)

As of April 30, 2025
1 Mo 3 Mo YTD 1 Yr 3 Yrs 5 Yrs 10 Yrs
Class A: NAV (Inception 07/09/12) 1.24 2.39 4.69 9.20 6.42 7.11 2.34
Class A: Maximum 5.75% load -4.58 -3.50 -1.33 2.92 4.34 5.85 1.73
Class I: NAV (Inception 07/09/12) 1.28 2.48 4.63 9.51 6.75 7.45 2.64
Class Y: NAV (Inception 07/09/12) 1.23 2.44 4.76 9.44 6.65 7.36 2.57
50% GBI-EM/50% EMBI 1.51 3.04 4.84 9.31 5.71 2.62 2.19

* Returns less than one year are not annualized.

Expenses: Class A: Gross 1.83%, Net 1.21%; Class I: Gross 1.37%, Net 0.86%; Class Y: Gross 1.33%, Net 0.96%. Expenses are capped contractually until 05/01/26 at 1.20% for Class A, 0.85% for Class I, 0.95% for Class Y. Caps exclude acquired fund fees and expenses, interest, trading, dividends, and interest payments of securities sold short, taxes, and extraordinary expenses.

The performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Performance may be lower or higher than performance data quoted. Please call 800.826.2333 or visit vaneck.com for performance current to the most recent month ended.

The “Net Asset Value” (NAV) of a Fund is determined at the close of each business day, and represents the dollar value of one share of the fund; it is calculated by taking the total assets of the fund, subtracting total liabilities, and dividing by the total number of shares outstanding. The NAV is not necessarily the same as the ETF’s intraday trading value. Investors should not expect to buy or sell shares at NAV.

Exposure Types and Significant Changes

The changes to our top positions are summarized below. Our largest positions in April were Mexico, Brazil, Thailand, South Africa, and Poland.

  • We increased our local currency exposure in Brazil, Thailand, and South Africa. Brazil shows signs of tentative fiscal improvement, its direct trade exposure to the U.S. is limited, inflation expectations had peaked, bringing us closer to the end of the tightening cycle. These factors improved the policy and technical test scores for the country. Thailand is a higher-yielding beneficiary of China’s restrained approach to the trade war, which improves its technical test score. South Africa’s budget debates are not over yet, but the main coalition partners confirmed that the government of national unity remains in place, improving the policy test score for the country.
  • We also increased our hard currency sovereign exposure in Ecuador, Argentina, and Nigeria. In Ecuador, the incumbent’s victory in the presidential elections should strengthen the reform mandate and the country’s policy test score. Argentina just got a new IMF program, which was conditional on a major overhaul of the country’s exchange rate regime with a freer floating currency and no FX intervention within the currency band. This boosted Argentina’s policy test score. We continue to see more green shoots in the Nigerian economy, and 80% of Nigeria’s exports is oil, which is exempt from the tariffs, which strengthens the economic and technical test scores for the country.
  • Finally, we increased our hard currency sovereign exposure in Gabon, Senegal, and Pakistan. In Gabon, we now have more certainty on the policy front after the former transitional president Nguema comfortably won the presidential election, improving the policy test score for the country.

    In Senegal, the IMF signaled that it is ready to move on a new program after the country addressed some data misreporting issues. This should strengthen the policy test score for Senegal. Pakistan’s valuations look better now, and the country’s direct exposure to the U.S. tariffs is limited. The U.S. is expected to keep Pakistan in its orbit for political reasons, and the IMF funding is expected to get unlocked after the board’s approval. In terms of our investment process, this improved the policy and technical test scores for the country.

  • We reduced our local currency exposure in Indonesia, Chile, Mexico, and Turkey. The Indonesian government has a communication problem, especially as regards its fiscal plans against the backdrop of downside growth risks.
